jokes.text: I think he'll be pretty impressed, knock on wood. [Michael knocks on what sounds like hollow wood/fake surface]
jokes.setup: Michael wants to show off their solid house construction to competitor
jokes.punchline: The house itself sounds flimsy when he knocks on it
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Michael's attempt to demonstrate quality immediately backfires. Economy is excellent - the joke works in just the sound effect.

jokes.text: Ann? Hmm. That'll play well with Sitwell. 'Where's your son?' 'He's out with Ann.' 'Who's Ann?' 'Don't ask me, I'm just the boy's father.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is dating someone named Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael realizes how pathetic it sounds that he doesn't know his son's girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Great character work showing Michael's self-awareness and the running gag of Ann being unmemorable. The dialogue structure builds perfectly to the punchline.

jokes.text: We know what we're doing. We don't need any help from him. [Clatters] Damn it. This thing got pushed in again.
jokes.setup: Gob declares they don't need help and know what they're doing
jokes.punchline: Immediately something breaks/malfunctions
jokes.explanation: Classic immediate contradiction setup. Works well but is fairly predictable structure-wise.

jokes.text: Say it in my own words, okay? Smart, but not too smart. Let's have a four-syllable max.
jokes.setup: Maeby needs help with a book report
jokes.punchline: She wants to fake intelligence with a syllable limit to avoid suspicion
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Maeby's calculated approach to academic dishonesty is both clever and funny. The specificity of 'four-syllable max' is brilliant.

jokes.text: They make a mistake once, they shan't make it twice. Oh, here I go!
jokes.setup: Tobias philosophizes about learning from mistakes
jokes.punchline: Immediately makes/repeats a mistake himself
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias - pompous wisdom immediately undercut by his own actions. The 'shan't' is perfect character voice.

jokes.text: Don't worry. It has not fallen into the garage. Knock on wood. [Clattering]
jokes.setup: Tobias reassures that nothing fell, knocks on wood for luck
jokes.punchline: Something immediately falls/crashes
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure - the 'knock on wood' phrase now has added ironic weight from the earlier scene.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? I didn't see the first Confidence Man. No. Confidence Man Two is my character.
jokes.setup: Michael assumes it's a sequel to 'Confidence Man'
jokes.punchline: It's actually the character name 'Confidence Man Two'
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection playing on sequel expectations. Economy is perfect - the misunderstanding unfolds naturally in just a few words.

jokes.text: Yes, well, it's for a con man, which I don't think you know anything about. And besides, don't you have school? No. Um- Today is 'Help Your Dad Follow His Dream' day.
jokes.setup: Tobias says Maeby doesn't know about con men and should be in school
jokes.punchline: Maeby immediately cons him with a fake school holiday
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Maeby proves she knows exactly how to con people by conning Tobias in the same breath. The fake holiday name is brilliantly specific.

jokes.text: The only help we need is from each other. And apparently whatever's in that bottle. Thanks for reminding me. My Teamocil.
jokes.setup: Michael declares family self-sufficiency
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately contradicts this by taking mood-altering supplements
jokes.explanation: Good immediate contradiction of Michael's statement, sets up the Teamocil subplot effectively.

jokes.text: [Narrator] In the mid '90s, Tobias formed a folk band to promote a supplement that promised better unity and teamwork. [Together] Teamocil - Teamocil may decrease your sex drive.
jokes.setup: Flashback to Tobias and Lindsay promoting teamwork supplement
jokes.punchline: The side effect warning contradicts the harmony they're singing about
jokes.explanation: Absurd concept of a folk band promoting pharmaceuticals, with the ironic side effect that would destroy their relationship harmony.

jokes.text: Since I've been taking this again, I've been getting along great with my husband. You're taking it for the side effect, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Lindsay praises how well she gets along with Tobias on Teamocil
jokes.punchline: She's actually taking it to kill her sex drive and avoid intimacy
jokes.explanation: Dark but brilliant - Lindsay's 'improvement' in her marriage is actually avoidance. Perfect character integration showing her dysfunction.

jokes.text: 'Sergeant Blank is treating me very blank and it looks like I'll be shipping off to blank in about a blank. I blank you, Mother. Buster.'
jokes.setup: Military letter from Buster is heavily censored
jokes.punchline: Even 'I love you' is censored, making communication absurdly meaningless
jokes.explanation: Absurd over-censorship creates humor through the contrast between expected family correspondence and military secrecy.

jokes.text: I even tried to convince them he's gay, but no one would believe that a woman like me would have a gay son.
jokes.setup: Lucille tried to get Buster out of military service
jokes.punchline: Her vanity prevents her from successfully claiming he's gay
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Lucille's narcissism - even her son's potential homosexuality becomes about her image. Dark but brilliantly character-specific.

jokes.text: Well, you certainly tried. You guys wore matching outfits till he was 12. [Narrator] Even once on a magazine cover that had been mocked within the family for years.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Lucille's close relationship with Buster
jokes.punchline: Visual reference to an embarrassing magazine cover of mother-son matching outfits
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup that adds to the family dysfunction. The narrator's addition about family mockery adds layers.

jokes.text: Lucille, I'll say it again. You never know what's going to happen in the army. Remember, Lucille? No, I don't.
jokes.setup: Oscar makes a meaningful reference to army experiences
jokes.punchline: Lucille's flat denial/refusal to remember
jokes.explanation: Lucille's selective memory and Oscar's loaded reference create tension and humor through what's unsaid.

jokes.text: Get me a vodka, rocks. Mom, it's breakfast. And a piece of toast.
jokes.setup: Lucille orders vodka, Michael objects it's breakfast
jokes.punchline: She adds toast as if that makes it a proper breakfast
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille moment - her solution to the breakfast objection is hilariously minimal and misses the point entirely.

jokes.text: That hairless freak is coming here? None of his hair is real, you know. You mean the guy we're meeting with can't even grow his own hair? Come on!
jokes.setup: Lucille insults Sitwell's hairlessness
jokes.punchline: Gob escalates the insult as if it's a business liability
jokes.explanation: Good character work with Lucille's casual cruelty and Gob's shallow business thinking. Sets up alopecia explanation.

jokes.text: Modest and generous? Then why is he always waving giant $10 million checks over his head every time some- Go ahead, Mom. Finish your thought. 'Every time some children's hospital needs funding'?
jokes.setup: Lucille starts to criticize Sitwell's check-waving
jokes.punchline: Michael forces her to complete the thought, revealing it's for charity
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - Lucille's complaint about Sitwell's generosity makes her look terrible. Michael's forcing the completion is perfect.

jokes.text: 'Every time some children's hospital needs funding'?
jokes.setup: Lucille starts to criticize Sitwell's charity work
jokes.punchline: Michael forces her to complete the obviously charitable thought
jokes.explanation: Great example of the family's cognitive dissonance - criticizing obvious good deeds.

jokes.text: Sorry that my son couldn't be here- Let me guess. Girlfriend. You know, it could be worse. He could wanna marry your mother. [Laughs] Oh, I'm sorry. Is your family not laughing at that yet?
jokes.setup: Sitwell makes what he thinks is a harmless joke about marrying mothers
jokes.punchline: References the actual Bluth family scandal with uncomfortable precision
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor that works on multiple levels - Sitwell accidentally hits too close to home, and his awareness of the awkwardness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You know, Sudden Valley conjures up the image of a sinkhole, no? I told that to Dad. I told that to my dad. I'm thinking- Paradise Gardens. [Wind Whistling] I swear to God, I have that written-
jokes.setup: Sitwell suggests Paradise Gardens as a better name than Sudden Valley
jokes.punchline: Wind sound effect suggests the location is actually terrible
jokes.explanation: Good irony - the 'Paradise Gardens' name is immediately undercut by ominous environmental sounds. Michael's validation adds character depth.

jokes.text: [Narrator] When George Sr. was in charge, he had a habit of shooting down any idea Michael came up with. And Sudden Valley just sort of implies that something awful could happen all of a sudden. Plus, it's on a hill. What are you taking, stupid pills? Come on.
jokes.setup: Flashback showing George Sr. dismissing Michael's concerns about the name
jokes.punchline: George's dismissive 'stupid pills' comment when Michael was actually right
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing George Sr.'s dismissive parenting style and Michael's vindication. Sets up family dynamic.

jokes.text: That's great. So the other 449 families live in fear? Is that what we're saying? I mean, come on! Where's your decen- What the hell just fell off your face?
jokes.setup: Michael gets worked up about housing proposal morality
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's fake eyebrow falls off mid-conversation
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy timing - the serious moral discussion is punctuated by absurd wig malfunction. Deflates tension effectively.

jokes.text: One of this guy's eyebrows fell in the candy beans. I always carry a spare. Well, I hope you also carry a spare bowl of candy beans.
jokes.setup: Sitwell matter-of-factly mentions carrying spare eyebrows
jokes.punchline: Gob's logical extension that he should carry spare candy too
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob logic - takes the absurd situation and makes it more absurd through seemingly reasonable extension.

jokes.text: Pretty good? It was horrible. What are you taking, stupid pills again? [Narrator] But their old dynamic returned when George Sr. did.
jokes.setup: Michael thinks he's impressing his father
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately dismisses him with the same 'stupid pills' line, showing nothing has changed
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that reinforces character dynamics. The narrator's observation makes the pattern explicit and sad.

jokes.text: [Narrator] While Stan Sitwell used the game to raise funds for underprivileged children. Oh, I think one of your orphans is heading towards the street. Strike three.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Sitwell's charitable softball games
jokes.punchline: George Sr. uses child safety as a distraction technique during the game
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing George Sr.'s willingness to exploit charitable context for competitive advantage. The casual cruelty is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: No hair. Hey, we got no hair.
jokes.setup: Flashback to George Sr. strategizing against Sitwell
jokes.punchline: His crude mockery of Sitwell's alopecia as gamesmanship
jokes.explanation: Shows George Sr.'s win-at-all-costs mentality, though the humor is somewhat crude.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. I must've put it on too high.
jokes.setup: Gob looks surprised when Sitwell offers him a job
jokes.punchline: He adjusts his eyebrows, treating facial expressions like costume pieces
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Gob moment - his vanity and stagecraft applied to basic human emotion.

jokes.text: You got gumption. You speak your mind. That means you're good enough to work for me. [Bleeps] you. And I accepted.
jokes.setup: Sitwell praises Gob for speaking his mind
jokes.punchline: Gob proves the point by telling Sitwell to [bleep] himself, then accepts the job
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob moment - his response is both exactly what Sitwell wants and completely inappropriate. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: It's ready, Mike. They're only gonna hold it for 24 hours.
jokes.setup: Gob is leaving to work for Sitwell
jokes.punchline: His priority is picking up Sitwell's 'just woke up' hair within the time limit
jokes.explanation: Absurd priority inversion - Gob treats the wig pickup as the urgent business matter. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Wait. You can't do a U-turn on your dream. I say, we get you on the lot and get the Fünke name out there. Start talking you up around the watercoolers.
jokes.setup: Tobias wants to leave after seeing the studio
jokes.punchline: Maeby's motivation speech uses business clichés to manipulate her father
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Maeby's manipulation skills and business savvy. The 'U-turn on your dream' is perfect teen speak.

jokes.text: What do you think of when you hear the words, 'Sudden Valley'? Salad dressing, I think. But for some reason, I don't wanna eat it. Right. But 'Paradise Gardens.' Yeah. Okay. I can see marinating a chicken in that.
jokes.setup: Michael tests the development names on George Michael
jokes.punchline: Both names sound like unappetizing food products to him
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing how both housing development names fail by sounding like food. George Michael's honest responses are perfectly naive.

jokes.text: [Narrator] We lost our entire outfield and a couple of court cases.
jokes.setup: Showing Gob's inappropriate behavior during previous softball games
jokes.punchline: The consequences escalated to actual legal trouble
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - losing players became losing court cases. Economy is excellent - maximum information in minimal words.

jokes.text: Plus, I got Aunt Lindsay playing this year. I can't very well pull her off the roster. Let's make Ann the backup, okay? Very good way to think about her... as a backup.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Ann as backup player
jokes.punchline: Michael's comment reveals his low opinion of Ann in general
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Ann's status in the family. Michael's casual dismissiveness perfectly captures his attitude.

jokes.text: Maybe-Maybe now I'll get a kiss. Okay. Oh. I meant from Ann. Yes, I know. I just want to get in there first.
jokes.setup: George Michael hopes for a kiss from Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael misunderstands and wants to kiss his son first, creating awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Awkward family moment with good misdirection. Michael's oversharing creates appropriate discomfort.

jokes.text: Oh. I meant from Ann.
jokes.setup: Michael says maybe now George Michael will get a kiss
jokes.punchline: George Michael has to clarify after Michael leans in
jokes.explanation: Peak awkward comedy - the uncomfortable family dynamic captured in perfect dialogue economy.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Lindsay felt the intended effect of Teamocil- a sense of camaraderie. Count me in, Michael. Happy to help the team.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Teamocil's intended effect
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's unusual helpfulness proves the drug is working
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Lindsay's later memory loss. The narrator's clinical observation contrasts with the family chaos.

jokes.text: Michael, I'm your big brother. I'll never be impressed with you.
jokes.setup: Michael wants to prove himself to Gob
jokes.punchline: Gob's honest admission that sibling rivalry is permanent
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest and perfectly captures toxic sibling dynamics. The economy is perfect - maximum emotional impact in minimal words.

jokes.text: Well, I just- Hey. Come on. I really want you to be over at Sitwell.
jokes.setup: Michael starts to explain his helpful motives
jokes.punchline: Accidentally reveals he wants Gob gone
jokes.explanation: Good character moment where Michael's true feelings slip out. The self-correction makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Croc! No, sorry. Wait. Log. Just a log. No, wait. Sorry. Croc!
jokes.setup: Oscar's job was spotting crocodiles in Vietnam
jokes.punchline: He's terrible at it, constantly changing his assessment
jokes.explanation: Great character consistency showing Oscar's incompetence. The rapid reversals create good physical comedy rhythm.

jokes.text: I prefer an older man. Hey, I'm free after the show. I said older, Bob, not dead.
jokes.setup: Young Lucille wants an older man
jokes.punchline: Brutally rejects the legendary Bob Hope as too old
jokes.explanation: Perfect young Lucille moment - cruel and specific. The Bob Hope reference adds historical comedy layer.

jokes.text: That Fünke is some kind of something. Boy, this Fünke is all anybody's ever talking about. So sick and tired of hearing about how brilliant that Fünke is. Overrated.
jokes.setup: Tobias is creating buzz about himself at watercoolers
jokes.punchline: He's having both sides of conversations and even criticizing himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Tobias delusion - he's so committed to the scheme he's creating negative buzz too. Perfect character psychology.

jokes.text: No. I'm just sitting behind someone else's desk pretending these are my kids.
jokes.setup: Maeby asks if it's his office
jokes.punchline: Casual admission he's pretending the family photos are his
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of an absurd situation. The casual admission makes it funnier than elaborate explanation.

jokes.text: Before gym-lunch... with Jim... Carrey. It's the Grinch guy. You're too young.
jokes.setup: Maeby tries to sound busy with celebrity meetings
jokes.punchline: Has to explain who Jim Carrey is, then gets dismissed as too young
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Maeby's attempt at adult business talk undermined by her actual age and cultural references.

jokes.text: You'd be amazed how fast they come out when you read them all in a row. 'Thirty-one, bamboo floor upgrades. Cheaper, stronger.' I can't read this word. 'Thirty-two, wireless 'crapability.''
jokes.setup: Gob is reading Michael's business proposals
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'capability' as 'crapability'
jokes.explanation: Simple malapropism that fits Gob's character. The 'explains itself' comment makes it slightly better.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And it ended as badly as any game of catch could end.
jokes.setup: Showing George Sr. playing catch with young Gob
jokes.punchline: Implied catastrophic outcome from simple father-son activity
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in the narrator's understatement. Leaves the disaster to imagination while implying it was spectacular.

jokes.text: White power! Better yet, maybe we can play ball on Saturday.
jokes.setup: Young Gob in a white baseball uniform
jokes.punchline: Innocent baseball cheer sounds like racist slogan
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from innocent context sounding sinister. Relies somewhat on shock value rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: [Narrator] Teamocil may cause numbness of the extremities. Why do you think I'm taking Teamocil? To curb your sex drive. That's right. What team are you talking about?
jokes.setup: Lindsay's foot is bleeding but she doesn't notice
jokes.punchline: Memory loss makes her forget the conversation mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Good payoff of established drug effects. Lindsay's immediate memory loss is perfectly timed for maximum impact.

jokes.text: Why do you think I'm taking Teamocil? To curb your sex drive. That's right. What team are you talking about?
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Lindsay will be able to play on the softball team
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's confused response showing short-term memory loss from Teamocil
jokes.explanation: Good payoff for the drug's side effects - Lindsay's confusion is both funny and concerning.

jokes.text: You know, she's got this low center of gravity. You can't knock her over. Well, I could knock her over. Dad, I'm telling you, you can't. They call her 'The Wall,' you know? Well, that's great. But I could knock her over. I won't, but I could.
jokes.setup: George Michael praises Ann's softball stability
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly defensive insistence he could knock her over
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Michael's need to assert dominance even over his son's girlfriend. 'The Wall' nickname is perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, God. That touch. Oh, how could I forget? Pat? Lucille. I could never forget that touch.
jokes.setup: Lucille touches the general's hand
jokes.punchline: He remembers her touch but gets her name wrong
jokes.explanation: Awkward romantic moment undercut by wrong name. Shows the general's selective/confused memory.

jokes.text: Maybe if you... go downtown. [Exhales] Oh, I haven't done that in 30 years.
jokes.setup: General asks if there's anything Lucille can do
jokes.punchline: Sexual innuendo about oral sex, Lucille's reluctant agreement
jokes.explanation: Innuendo works for character development but relies on sexual suggestion rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: What's your name? Tobias. He's too short. Give it to the guard.
jokes.setup: Tobias finally gets to audition
jokes.punchline: Instantly rejected for height before he can perform
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Tobias's hopes. The casual brutality and guard reference work well together.

jokes.text: You're the Fünke everyone's talking about? What are you, like, 15? Marry me. [Chuckles] Everyone thinks I look young too.
jokes.setup: Executive discovers Maeby is the famous Fünke
jokes.punchline: Proposing to who he thinks is a 15-year-old, she misunderstands the context
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable humor from the age misunderstanding. Maeby's response shows her naivety about the implications.

jokes.text: Yeah. Go young. Young Guy and the Sea. Big spring-breaker. Four C.G.I. The fish. Let's fast-track this one.
jokes.setup: Executive wants to adapt 'The Old Man and the Sea'
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstands the classic with terrible Hollywood changes
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of Hollywood's tendency to ruin classics. 'Four C.G.I.' and 'spring-breaker' are perfectly awful suggestions.

jokes.text: Listen to me. Brand new cars don't get waxed!
jokes.setup: Gob is angry about something being 'all waxed up'
jokes.punchline: His indignation about car waxing reveals shallow priorities
jokes.explanation: Good Gob moment showing his focus on appearance over substance. The car analogy is perfectly shallow.

jokes.text: After that list of ideas you gave me, I don't care if you blow the whole season. Come here, you.
jokes.setup: Gob is playing poorly
jokes.punchline: Sitwell's support makes Gob feel guilty about throwing the game
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Sitwell's unexpected kindness changing Gob's motivation. The irony works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Stop with the Tweety Bird dance, you moron. Dad? He made the call. You're out. What are you doing? Shush, shush, shush. Dad?
jokes.setup: George Sr. appears at the softball game
jokes.punchline: Gob's confusion between his celebration dance and his father's presence
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier established character trait. The family reunion moment adds emotional complexity.

jokes.text: I'm proud of you. Really? Yeah. See ya.
jokes.setup: Michael finally stands up to his father
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s approval comes with immediate abandonment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures the toxic father-son dynamic - approval followed by immediate emotional withdrawal. Economy is masterful.

jokes.text: Or not. I think hallucination is one of the side-effects of Teamocil. [Narrator] It isn't.
jokes.setup: Michael thinks he imagined seeing his father
jokes.punchline: Narrator's correction implies George Sr. was really there
jokes.explanation: Perfect narrator intervention. The deadpan correction adds both humor and plot information efficiently.

jokes.text: [Whistling Through Teeth] I miss him so much.
jokes.setup: Gob acts tough about his father
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits he misses him desperately
jokes.explanation: Perfect emotional vulnerability from Gob. The teeth-whistling detail adds character specificity to the moment.

jokes.text: Andy Richter! Here are those head shots I was telling you about. Yeah. I gotta go. It's Tobias Fünke. I told you about the head shots?
jokes.setup: Tobias gets excited seeing Andy Richter
jokes.punchline: Richter clearly wants to escape and doesn't remember Tobias
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity cameo showing Hollywood's treatment of wannabes. Tobias's desperation is well-captured.

jokes.text: I just wanted to thank you for my new job. [Giggles] What's wrong? I'm shipping out tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Buster thanks the general for getting him out of combat
jokes.punchline: He's actually being shipped to the front lines
jokes.explanation: Good irony showing Lucille's plan backfired. Buster's innocent gratitude makes it darker.

jokes.text: Oh. I haven't felt hands like that in years. You are coming to the front.
jokes.setup: General enjoys Buster's massage
jokes.punchline: Double meaning of 'coming to the front' - both massage and deployment
jokes.explanation: Decent double entendre that works on both levels. Not particularly clever but serves the plot.
